journal_entry.description: Borrowed $200,000 from bank at 6% annual interest for 3 years, interest payable semi-annually
journal_entry.rationale: Cash received from bank loan increases the asset account Cash (debit). The obligation to repay the principal creates a liability, recorded as Loan Payable or Bank Loan (credit). The transaction describes the receipt of loan proceeds; interest expense will be recorded separately when accrued or paid. This is a straightforward financing transaction with no tax implications (loan proceeds are not taxable income, and borrowing is not subject to HST/GST). The debit tuple (1,0,0,0,0,0) correctly identifies one asset increase (Cash), and the credit tuple (1,0,0,0,0,0) correctly identifies one liability increase (Loan Payable).

output.reason: Atlas Van Lines receives $200,000 cash from the bank. Cash is an asset, and receiving cash increases the asset. This is 1 debit line: asset increase. The interest rate and payment terms affect future transactions but not the initial borrowing entry.

debit_classifier.reason: Atlas Van Lines receives $200,000 cash from the bank. Cash is an asset, and receiving cash increases the asset. This is 1 debit line: asset increase. The interest rate and payment terms affect future transactions but not the initial borrowing entry.

output.reason: Atlas Van Lines borrowed $200,000 from the bank. This creates a loan payable (liability). On the credit side, we record the liability increase. There is one credit line: the bank loan payable = liability increase.

credit_classifier.reason: Atlas Van Lines borrowed $200,000 from the bank. This creates a loan payable (liability). On the credit side, we record the liability increase. There is one credit line: the bank loan payable = liability increase.

output.reason: Entry correctly records the loan proceeds. Cash (asset) debited for $200,000, Loan Payable (liability) credited for $200,000. Balance verified (debits = credits). The interest rate and payment terms are noted but correctly excluded from the initial borrowing entry - interest will be recorded when accrued or paid per IFRS. No tax implications for loan proceeds. All accounts appropriate and amounts match transaction text.
